retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Cochlear Function -- Related Testing
retrieved_docs.content: Electrocochleography is a test that records the electrical potentials generated by the cochlea and the vestibulocochlear nerve (CN VIII). An electrode is placed in the ear canal, on the tympanic membrane, or through the tympanic membrane on the cochlea. It measures the summating potential generated by hair cells of the cochlea and the action potentials of the cochlear nerve in response to acoustic stimulation. Electrocochleography is most useful in diagnosing endolymphatic hydrops, commonly seen in Ménierè disease, and for intraoperative monitoring of the cochlea and CN VIII. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Electrocochleography -- Potential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: ECochG monitoring via ET electrode may be used to assess whether the electrode has traversed the cochlea's basilar membrane during cochlear implant insertion and to assess residual hearing. However, correlations between ECochG and pure-tone audiograms have demonstrated significant variability concerning postoperative results. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Electrocochleography -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Electrocochleography (ECochG) stands at the forefront of diagnostic audiology, offering a unique window into the electrical activity of the inner ear. This 30-minute test involves electrode placement on the scalp and within the ear canal or through the tympanic membrane, capturing the cochlear microphonic (CM), action potential (AP), summation potential (SP), and auditory nerve neurophonic (ANN). In this activity, participants will delve into the pivotal role of the interprofessional team in evaluating and treating patients undergoing ECochG, exploring its safety, reliability, and applications in diagnosing a spectrum of hearing disorders, including Ménière's disease and auditory neuropathy. Recent advancements in ECochG technology are scrutinized, particularly its role in real-time feedback during cochlear implantation surgeries.

retrieved_docs.title: Electrocochleography -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: ECochG comprises 3 potentials: the cochlear microphonic (CM), the action potential (AP), and the summation potential (SP) (see Image. Electrocochleography Diaphragm). [1] The CM is an alternating current resembling the waveform of the stimulus. It is primarily generated by the outer hair cells. The quality of the CM recording is highly variable and may be easily confused with stimulus artifacts. [1] Production of an artifact is much less likely when the stimulation source is intracochlear (ie, via a cochlear implant), and the CM may demonstrate post-implantation changes after insertion. Furthermore, the real-time feedback offered by ECochG during cochlear implantation has been used to ensure optimal placement of the implant electrodes, minimize trauma to the cochlear structures, and preserve residual hearing.

retrieved_docs.title: Electrocochleography -- Normal and Critical Findings
retrieved_docs.content: Sound is transmitted from the tympanic membrane to the inner ear via ossicular chain movement. The vibration of the stapes footplate on the oval window creates a hydromechanical wave of perilymph in the cochlea, which generates vibration at different points on the basilar membrane, depending on the frequency of the sound stimulus. Basilar membrane movement causes the corresponding movement of hair cell stereocilia, thereby opening ion channels and converting mechanical energy into electrical voltage. This voltage elicits neurotransmitter release in the spiral ganglion and results in the perception of sound. The CM represents the alternating current voltage caused by the outer hair cells' potassium and calcium ion influx in response to an auditory stimulus.

retrieved_docs.title: Electrocochleography -- Potential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: The primary condition diagnosed and monitored by ECochG is MD/ELH. Intraoperative nerve monitoring also remains an important indication for ECochG, particularly in removing cerebellopontine angle tumors. In the context of cochlear implantation, ECochG provides valuable real-time feedback regarding the functional integrity of the cochlea, assisting surgeons in making critical intraoperative decisions. ECochG may also be used to estimate hearing thresholds, but more convenient testing methods, such as ABR, have largely been replaced in this indication. While the reproducibility, specificity, and sensitivity of ET recordings remain poor, an SP:AP ratio ≥0.45 is nevertheless a significant diagnostic indicator of MD. [11] As there is substantial variability with both recordings, particularly with the SP, the SP:AP ratio must be interpreted cautiously and in the appropriate clinical context.

retrieved_docs.title: Electrocochleography -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: ECochG is a helpful tool for evaluating inner ear fluid distortion and increased pressure in the MD/ELH setting. It additionally may be used to help assess hearing thresholds and auditory neuropathy, as ECochG has been demonstrated to correlate with pure-tone thresholds, albeit with some variability. Recently, other testing methods, such as ABR, have largely replaced ECochG in specific clinical settings due to patient tolerance and ease of use. ECochG has become less commonly utilized in the evaluation of MD/ELH. However, with the growing number of cochlear implantations performed, the role of ECochG in intraoperative neuromonitoring has become increasingly significant. ECochG facilitates optimal electrode placement, potentially reducing postoperative complications and ensuring the best possible outcomes for the patient. [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Electrocochleography -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The SP is a direct current potential resulting from signal transduction initiated by the vibration of the basilar membrane. The SP is clinically most important concerning the AP, as the SP:AP amplitude ratio is often used to diagnose patients with Ménière disease (MD) or endolymphatic hydrops (ELH). [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Electrocochleography -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Electrocochleography (ECochG) is a testing procedure that enables the clinician to assess cochlear electrical potentials. While it can be challenging to perform and has been largely replaced by more convenient tests, it continues to be used in several clinical scenarios for diagnostic and intraoperative purposes. In recent years, advances in technology and a greater understanding of cochlear physiology have further enhanced the utility of ECochG, particularly in monitoring the functional status of the cochlea during cochlear implantation surgeries.

retrieved_docs.title: Brainstem Auditory Evoked Response Test -- Issues of Concern -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: The auditory pathway begins with the cochlea within the inner ear. The cochlear hair cells convert mechanical sound vibrations into electrical action potentials. These action potentials are transmitted through the auditory nerve, one of the two branches of the vestibulocochlear nerve (cranial nerve VIII). [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Perilymphatic Fistula -- Evaluation -- Audiological Studies
retrieved_docs.content: Numerous audiological studies may be employed to provide additional diagnostic information before undertaking surgical confirmation and repair. Audiometry is needed to determine the type and degree of hearing loss, if any is present, and video or electro-nystagmography will quantify any vestibular hypofunction. [27] Electrocochleography often demonstrates an elevated summating potential (SP) to action potential (AP) ratio if a PLF is present, similar to results in cases of Ménière disease, although the SP increases in Ménière disease, while the AP may decrease with a labyrinthine fistula.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Cochlear Function -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: Approximately 95% of spiral ganglion neurons synapse on IHCs. The remaining 5-10% of spiral ganglion neurons innervate the OHCs. The OHCs increase the maximum amplitude of the traveling wave of vibration. Efferent nerve fibers from the brain synapse on OHCs and decrease their ability to enhance the amplitude of the vibrational wave. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Electrocochleography -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Participants will gain a comprehensive understanding of the intricacies of ECochG, examining the interpretation of APs with a focus on N1 and N2 peaks and the clinical significance of the SP:AP amplitude ratio in the diagnosis of Ménière disease and endolymphatic hydrops. The activity will also shed light on the ANN, its frequency variations with stimulus frequency, and its role as a reliable indicator of auditory neuropathy. This activity empowers healthcare professionals with the knowledge to effectively integrate ECochG into clinical practice and enhance patient outcomes in auditory diagnostics and intervention.

retrieved_docs.title: Electrocochleography -- Procedures
retrieved_docs.content: While TT ECochG has significant accuracy benefits over ET approaches due to the proximity of the recording electrodes to the signal-generating anatomical structures, it requires physician monitoring and patient cooperation and is challenging to perform in most clinical settings. [1] TT ECochG remains useful in certain intraoperative situations and is generally considered a more accurate recording method than ET ECochG. Nevertheless, TT ECochG has certain limitations beyond patient tolerance. Firstly, the needle risks penetration of an abnormally positioned round window in cochlear malformations. [8] Secondly, the high impedance factor of a needle may exclude lower action potential frequencies. TT ECochG is particularly helpful in the intraoperative setting because it provides precise neuromonitoring without concern for patient compliance. Local or topical anesthetics may improve patient tolerance in clinical settings. Three primary methods of stimulus are presented: clicks, tone bursts, and chirps. [6] Click stimuli assess a large population of neurons with a single, brief stimulus, while tonal stimuli are more precisely aligned with audiometric thresholds, especially at 1,000 Hz, 2,000 Hz, and 4,000 Hz; chirp usage is less common. The application of each method remains controversial and is dependent on clinician preference.

retrieved_docs.title: Electrocochleography -- Normal and Critical Findings
retrieved_docs.content: The SP represents the sum of transduction processes in the cochlea, from the inner hair cells, outer hair cells, and auditory nerve. The SP magnitude corresponds to the amount of distortion in these processes. Consequently, the SP may be enlarged in situations with increased distortion, such as in ELH, where pressure is increased relative to patients with normal inner ear and temporal bone anatomy. The AP, specifically its N1 peak, has 2 main characteristics that are clinically relevant: latency and magnitude. Latency describes the time duration from stimulus to N1 peak, while magnitude correlates with the number of firing nerve fibers. Thus, while the SP magnitude increases with distortion in the transduction process, the AP will remain relatively constant. This results in an increased SP:AP ratio, which is pathognomonic for ELH. [1] The AP also has a second peak (N2), which corresponds to ABR Wave II, although there is currently no known clinical significance associated with the N2 peak of the AP. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Sensorineural Hearing Loss -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Each of the above components is important for the conduction of sound waves, but in SNHL, we are concerned with pathology in the inner ear that leads to hearing loss. The interface between the stapes and the oval window delivers sound transmission to the cochlea. Sound which reaches the cochlea undergoes first amplification by the outer hair cells and then electrochemical transduction by the inner hair cells. The cochlea receives an acoustic signal, and a traveling wave is generated, which traverses the basilar membrane of the cochlea stimulating outer hair cells (OHCs), which act as a biological amplifier/compressor and modifies the signal. The basilar membrane of the cochlea is highly frequency-specific and tonotopically organized. The base of the basilar membrane responds to higher-frequency sounds, while the apex responds to lo frequencies. [10] The inner hair cells (IHCs) in the cochlea transduce the energy of the traveling wave to an electric action potential and synapse at the spiral ganglion to form the auditory nerve. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 3 -- Community 2664
retrieved_docs.content: Elektrostimulační terapie, a therapeutic modality that applies electrical currents to stimulate nerves, muscles, or other tissues, is broadly related to إرحال أيوني, reflecting its influence on ionic transport processes within cells and across membranes. This relationship highlights how electrostimulation can modulate ion channel activity, alter membrane potentials, and affect physiological functions such as muscle contraction, nerve conduction, and tissue repair. Recognizing the connection between elektrostimulační terapie and إرحال أيوني is essential for optimizing treatment protocols, anticipating therapeutic outcomes, and integrating electrostimulation strategies into comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2239
retrieved_docs.content: The electrocardiogram, a diagnostic test that records the heart’s electrical activity, is classified in Arabic as تخطيط كهربية القلب, reflecting its direct translation and terminology across languages.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2401
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiovascular examination and evaluation encompasses a broad set of cardiovascular procedures, each representing a specific therapeutic or diagnostic technique. Among these procedures are cardioversion by intracardiac catheter, which uses an internal catheter to deliver electrical shocks for rhythm conversion; cardioversion, elective, electrical conversion of arrhythmia; external, which involves non‑invasive electrical shock delivery to restore normal cardiac rhythm; demand pacing, a form of cardiac pacing that provides electrical stimulation only when the heart’s intrinsic rate falls below a preset threshold; stimulation of the carotid sinus with simultaneous electrocardiographic monitoring, a maneuver that assesses autonomic reflexes and cardiac response by mechanically stimulating the carotid sinus while recording ECG changes; and plethysmografie celotělová (whole‑body plethysmography), a respiratory measurement technique that can be incorporated into cardiovascular assessments to evaluate pulmonary contributions to cardiac function. Together, these child procedures define the scope of cardiovascular examination and evaluation, illustrating the interrelated therapeutic and diagnostic options available for comprehensive cardiac care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 4 -- Community 2879
retrieved_docs.content: Electrocardiographic and Monitoring Procedures represent a comprehensive group of cardiac assessment techniques that include Cardiotachometer monitoring, Intracardiac phonocardiogram, Monitoring of pacemaker (regime/therapy), Monitoring of pulse, and Phonocardiogram with apex cardiogram. Cardiotachometer monitoring is incorporated within this broader category, providing rapid measurement of heart rate and rhythm. Intracardiac phonocardiogram records internal heart sounds to evaluate valve function and intracavitary flow. Monitoring of pacemaker (regime/therapy) tracks the performance and settings of implanted pacing devices to ensure appropriate cardiac support. Monitoring of pulse offers non‑invasive assessment of peripheral pulse characteristics, reflecting cardiac output and rhythm. Phonocardiogram with apex cardiogram combines surface heart sound recording with apex cardiography to analyze both acoustic and mechanical cardiac events. Together, these procedures enable clinicians to obtain detailed electrical, mechanical, and hemodynamic information essential for diagnosing and managing a wide range of cardiac disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 579
retrieved_docs.content: procedures that also encompass general diagnostic procedures on the heart and pericardium. These diagnostic activities are associated with cardiac surgical operations (العمليات الجراحية في القلب). Electrocardiographic assessment (تخطيط كهربية القلب ECG) and electrographic cardiac monitoring are directly related to the heart’s electrical evaluation. Device‑related interventions are classified under the comprehensive category “Insertion; revision; replacement; removal of cardiac pacemaker or cardioverter/defibrillator.” This category includes implantation of a rate‑responsive single‑chamber cardiac device, initial implantation of a cardiac single‑ chamber device, removal of a cardiac pacemaker with replacement by a single‑chamber device, removal of pacemaker electrode leads without replacement, and replacement of a cardiac pacemaker with a dual‑chamber device. From a systemic perspective, the condition “Risk for ineffective cerebral tissue perfusion” is associated with peripheral and

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 1031
retrieved_docs.content: the removal of a cardiac pacemaker with replacement by a single‑ chamber device, the removal of pacemaker electrode leads without replacement, and the replacement of a cardiac pacemaker with a dual‑chamber device all have the heart as an indirect procedure site. These procedures are collectively classified under the category “Insertion; revision; replacement; removal of cardiac pacemaker or cardioverter/defibrillator,” indicating that each specific intervention (implantation, removal, replacement) falls within this broader procedural taxonomy. In addition to device‑related interventions, several monitoring activities are performed directly on the heart. Monitoring of ECG at surgery, combined monitoring of ECG, pressure, blood gases and cardiac output, and transesophageal echocardiographic monitoring all list the heart as a direct procedure site, reflecting the importance of real‑time functional assessment during cardiac care. Overall, the knowledge base links the heart to chronic

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 170
retrieved_docs.content: (including simultaneous measurement of pressure, blood gases, and cardiac output) and enhances diagnostic precision when integrated with nuclear imaging, electrophysiologic testing, and autonomic stimulation. ECG arrhythmia monitors and non‑sinus‑rhythm ECGs are linked to conduction disorders, illustrating the integration of ECG findings with the management of cardiac conduction problems. In cardiac device therapy, insertion, revision, replacement, and removal of cardiac pacemakers or cardioverter‑defibrillators—including implantation of rate‑responsive single‑chamber devices, replacement with dual‑chamber devices, removal of pacemaker leads without replacement, and removal of a pacemaker with replacement by a rate‑responsive single‑chamber device—are grouped under a comprehensive procedural category. Excision of a heart valve falls under HEART: EXCISIONS, representing valve removal as an excisional cardiac procedure. Collectively, these entities illustrate an interconnected framework

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1783
retrieved_docs.content: Electrographic cardiac monitoring, referred to in Arabic as تخطيط كهربية القلب ECG, is a diagnostic technique that records the electrical activity of the heart to assess cardiac rhythm and function. This monitoring method provides clinicians with real‑time information about cardiac electrophysiology, aiding in the detection of arrhythmias, myocardial ischemia, and other cardiac abnormalities. By establishing a direct relationship between the monitoring process and the ECG trace, healthcare providers can evaluate heart health, guide treatment decisions, and improve patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1862
retrieved_docs.content: Electrocardiographic and Monitoring Procedures is a comprehensive diagnostic category that encompasses several specialized techniques. Within this category, Cardiotachometry is used to assess heart rate and rhythm through pulse‑based measurements. Intracardiac phonocardiogram provides acoustic recordings from within the cardiac chambers to evaluate heart sounds directly. Monitoring of pulse is a fundamental component for evaluating circulatory status and detecting abnormalities in heart rhythm. Phonocardiogram with apex cardiogram records heart sounds at the cardiac apex, offering detailed information about valvular function and cardiac mechanics. Phonocardiogram with artery and/or vein tracing combines heart‑sound analysis with simultaneous arterial or venous waveform monitoring, allowing integrated assessment of cardiac and vascular dynamics. Together, these procedures constitute the full spectrum of electrocardiographic and monitoring methods employed in cardiac evaluation and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 170
retrieved_docs.content: around the heart and pericardium. “Other or heart procedures” and the broader category HEART: GENERAL AND MISCELLANEOUS OPERATIVE PROCEDURES include cardiac pacing (demand pacing, implantation of rate‑responsive single‑chamber devices, replacement with dual‑chamber devices, and lead removal), cardioversion (intracardiac‑catheter and elective external electrical), carotid‑sinus stimulation with simultaneous electrocardiographic monitoring, temporary cardio‑assist with an artificial heart, whole‑body plethysmography, and open‑heart surgery. Diagnostic cardiac catheterization, coronary arteriography and related diagnostic cardiovascular procedures form the parent categories for “Diagnostic procedures on heart and pericardium,” which encompass heart biopsy and its sub‑procedures (open left‑ventricular biopsy, open right‑ventricular biopsy, transluminal left‑ventricular biopsy). Heart biopsy serves as a central hub linking diagnostic interventions to HEART: EXCISIONS and to the inverse‑ISA

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2627
retrieved_docs.content: Electrocardiographic and Monitoring Procedures constitute a broad category of cardiovascular assessment techniques that include several specific monitoring methods. Blood pressure monitoring and cardiotachometer monitoring are sub‑procedures within this category, providing continuous measurement of arterial pressure and heart rate, respectively. The category also encompasses intracardiac phonocardiogram, a specialized technique for recording heart sounds from within the cardiac chambers, as well as monitoring of pacemaker regime or therapy, which involves tracking the function and settings of implanted pacemakers. Additionally, monitoring of pulse is included as a fundamental component of these procedures, allowing clinicians to evaluate rhythm and peripheral perfusion. Together, these interconnected monitoring modalities support comprehensive evaluation and management of cardiac function in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 3 -- Community 2595
retrieved_docs.content: Electrocardiographic and Monitoring Procedures is a subcategory of Cardiovascular and Respiratory System Procedures and comprises a range of cardiac and respiratory monitoring techniques. Within this category, Blood pressure monitoring, Cardiotachometer monitoring, and Cardiotachometry are identified as specific child procedures that provide quantitative assessments of cardiovascular function. Additionally, Electrocardiographic and Monitoring Procedures includes several phonocardiographic methods as child procedures: Intracardiac phonocardiogram, Phonocardiogram with apex cardiogram, and Phonocardiogram with artery and/or vein tracing. Together, these procedures form an integrated set of diagnostic tools used to evaluate electrical activity, hemodynamic parameters, and acoustic signals of the heart and related vascular structures, supporting comprehensive patient assessment in cardiology and respiratory medicine.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2254
retrieved_docs.content: Electrocardiographic and Monitoring Procedures constitute a comprehensive category of diagnostic and therapeutic monitoring techniques that encompass several specific modalities. Blood pressure monitoring and cardiotachometry are identified as child procedures within this category, reflecting their roles in assessing circulatory dynamics and cardiac rate respectively. Additional child procedures include monitoring of pacemaker regime or therapy, which evaluates the function and settings of implanted cardiac devices; monitoring of pulse, which provides real‑time assessment of arterial pulse characteristics; and phonocardiogram with artery and/or vein tracing, which records acoustic heart sounds together with vascular flow patterns. Together, these interconnected procedures enable clinicians to obtain detailed cardiovascular information, guide treatment decisions, and ensure optimal patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 185
retrieved_docs.content: structural heart changes, electrophysiologic abnormalities, diagnostic terminologies, monitoring technologies, interventional procedures, pharmacologic considerations, and systemic organ interactions essential for accurate diagnosis, risk stratification, and holistic cardiac patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2636
retrieved_docs.content: The electrocardiogram, also referred to in Arabic as تخطيط كهربية القلب, is classified as an electrocardiogram rhythm‑strip telephonic or telemetric transmission. This form of transmission is specifically focused on قياس القلب عن بعد, meaning remote measurement of the heart. By delivering rhythm‑strip data through telephone or telemetry channels, the electrocardiogram enables clinicians to assess cardiac electrical activity and heart function from a distance, supporting remote cardiac monitoring and diagnosis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2064
retrieved_docs.content: Electrocardiographic and Monitoring Procedures represent a comprehensive category of cardiovascular assessment techniques that include blood pressure monitoring, cardiotachometry, intracardiac phonocardiogram, monitoring of pacemaker (regime/therapy), and monitoring of pulse. Blood pressure monitoring provides continuous or intermittent measurement of arterial pressure, aiding in the detection of hypertension and hemodynamic instability. Cardiotachometry records the speed of cardiac impulses, offering insight into conduction velocity and rhythm disturbances. Intracardiac phonocardiogram captures acoustic signals from within the heart, allowing detailed evaluation of intracardiac sounds and murmurs. Monitoring of pacemaker (regime/therapy) tracks device function, battery status, and pacing parameters to ensure appropriate therapy delivery. Monitoring of pulse assesses peripheral pulse rate and rhythm, contributing to the overall evaluation of cardiac output and circulatory status. Together, these procedures form an integrated monitoring framework that supports thorough cardiac diagnostics, guides therapeutic decisions, and enhances patient safety in clinical practice.
